name: Arms Transfers to Iran — Fighter Aircraft
description: Russia signed a contract for 48 Su-35 flanker-E multirole fighters with Iran, with delivery scheduled across 2026–2028. At least one squadron of Yak-130 advanced jet trainers has been delivered, along with up to 6 Mi-28 attack helicopters by January 2026. A €500 million deal for 500 Verba MANPADS was signed in December 2025. These transfers significantly upgrade Iran's aging air force (which still relies on pre-1979 F-4s, F-5s, and F-14s) and its ground-based air defense. In an Iran-US conflict scenario, these systems would improve Iranian capacity to contest airspace and attrit US standoff platforms, though the Su-35s are not yet delivered in sufficient numbers to be operationally decisive. Russia's hard limit on transfers is that both countries consume the same weapons categories — air defense, ballistic missiles, drones — and Russia's own defense industrial capacity is severely strained by Ukraine, constraining large-scale wartime resupply.
unit: Su-35 fighters (contracted, delivery 2026–2028); 500 Verba MANPADS (contracted Dec 2025); ~6 Mi-28 helicopters (delivered); 1 sqn Yak-130 (delivered)
political_cost: Russia cannot spare frontline combat aircraft or advanced air defense systems from Ukraine without accepting territorial losses on its primary front, meaning direct military transfers are constrained by industrial competition for the same munitions categories.
source: IISS Military Balance; ISW assessments; Columbia CGEP; project knowledge verified arms transfer data

name: Arms Transfers to Iran — Air Defense Systems
description: Russia delivered S-300PMU-2 systems to Iran beginning in 2016, providing Iran's most capable long-range SAM coverage until these systems were heavily targeted and degraded during the June 2025 Israeli strikes. Russia has subsequently transferred S-400 components to reconstitute Iranian air defense, along with the Rezonans-NE over-the-horizon radar system. Iran's air defenses were assessed as severely degraded after June 2025 but were being rebuilt with Russian assistance through the interwar period (July 2025–January 2026). In a conflict scenario, even partially reconstituted S-400 coverage would force US/Israeli aircraft to operate at standoff ranges, increasing reliance on expensive cruise missiles and reducing sortie effectiveness. The key constraint is that Russia itself faces acute air defense shortages along its own frontlines in Ukraine and cannot transfer complete S-400 batteries without creating gaps in its own integrated air defense network.
unit: S-400 components (partial delivery); S-300PMU-2 batteries (pre-existing, degraded); Rezonans-NE radar
political_cost: Transferring complete S-400 batteries would leave Russian critical infrastructure and frontline positions exposed; Russia will provide components and technical assistance but not strip its own IADS.
source: IISS; CSIS; ISW; project knowledge verified transfer data

name: UAV Technology Sharing and Drone Modifications
description: Russia and Iran have developed a deep bidirectional drone technology relationship. Iran supplied Russia with Shahed-136 one-way attack drones for use in Ukraine beginning in 2022, and Russia has since combat-tested and iteratively improved these designs over nearly four years of continuous warfare. By January 2026, Russia is sharing improved Shahed modifications back to Iran, including better targeting algorithms, enhanced GPS/INS guidance, improved handling characteristics, and optimized flight profiles derived from real combat data against Western air defenses. Israeli intelligence assessed Iran's Shahed surge production capacity at approximately 400 units per day with Russian technical support, against a peacetime baseline of 200–500 per month. This technology feedback loop is one of the most operationally significant forms of Russian support — it allows Iran to field a cheap, mass-produced drone force that imposes asymmetric cost ratios of 100:1 to 350:1 against Western interceptors.
unit: technical assistance, flight profile data, targeting improvements
political_cost: Drone technology sharing is deniable and low-cost; Russia faces no meaningful political threshold for continuing this support since the technology transfer is bidirectional and both countries benefit.
source: CSIS; Bloomberg; Israeli intelligence assessments; ISW; project knowledge drone stockpile data

name: Satellite Imagery and ISR Support via Khayyam/Kanopus-V
description: Russia launched the Khayyam satellite (a Kanopus-V variant re-designated for Iranian operations) providing Iran with round-the-clock optical and radar imagery. This gives Iran access to Russian reconnaissance constellation data, including the ability to track movements of US warships, aircraft carrier strike groups, and forward-deployed military assets across the Persian Gulf and wider CENTCOM AOR. As of January 2026, this capability is operational and represents a standing intelligence-sharing arrangement. In a conflict, this satellite ISR would enable Iran to target US naval assets with anti-ship missiles (Noor, Qader, Khalij Fars) and time drone swarm launches for maximum effect against dispersed US positions. The political significance is that this is a force-multiplier that Russia provides at zero military risk — no Russian personnel are in theater, and the data transfer is technically deniable at the tactical level even if strategically acknowledged.
unit: Khayyam reconnaissance satellite plus access to broader Russian constellation
political_cost: Intelligence sharing via satellite is the lowest-risk, highest-leverage form of support; Russia would escalate to real-time tactical targeting data only if US actions directly threatened Russian strategic interests (e.g., strikes on Russian advisors or assets).
source: Washington Post (multiple US officials); CNN; NBC News; AP; project knowledge verified intelligence data

name: UN Security Council Veto and Diplomatic Cover
description: Russia holds a permanent UNSC veto and has consistently used it to shield Iran from binding resolutions imposing new sanctions or authorizing the use of force. Russia coordinates closely with China at the UNSC to present a unified bloc against Western-sponsored Iran resolutions, ensuring that any military action against Iran lacks UN authorization and can be framed as illegal under international law. This diplomatic cover is strategically valuable because it delegitimizes US military operations in the eyes of the Global South, NAM states, and BRICS partners. Russia amplifies this framing through bilateral statements, SCO joint declarations, and coordinated media narratives positioning any US-Iran conflict as Western aggression. The cost to Russia is near-zero — vetoes are costless in terms of material resources and align with Russia's broader narrative of opposing a 'rules-based order' that it characterizes as US hegemonic architecture.
unit: permanent UNSC veto
political_cost: UNSC vetoes are politically costless for Russia and align perfectly with its existing narrative framework; the threshold for use is any Western-sponsored resolution on Iran.
source: UN records; IISS Strategic Survey; RUSI analysis

name: Oil Market Beneficiary — Revenue Windfall from Iran Conflict
description: An Iran-US conflict is Russia's most favorable geopolitical scenario economically. Pre-conflict, Russia's oil exports were at their weakest since the 2022 Ukraine invasion — $9.5 billion in February 2026 with Urals crude at approximately $40/barrel, roughly $30 below Brent. Russia's 2026 budget assumes $59/barrel. Any Strait of Hormuz disruption would spike global oil prices dramatically — Goldman Sachs projected $120–130/barrel for a full blockade scenario. Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center analyst Sergey Vakulenko estimates each $10/month increase generates approximately $1.63 billion in additional Russian state revenue. At $90/barrel sustained, Russia could see roughly $55 billion in additional annual revenue. This windfall directly funds Russia's war effort in Ukraine, offsets sanctions damage, and creates a massive incentive for Russia to prolong rather than resolve any Iran-US conflict. Russia produced 9.87 million bpd in 2025, making it the world's second-largest producer.
unit: million barrels per day (2025 production); $1.63B additional revenue per $10/barrel increase
political_cost: Russia has no political cost from passively benefiting from oil price spikes; the threshold for concern would only be if oil prices rose so high that Russian domestic consumers faced inflation beyond the regime's tolerance.
source: Carnegie Russia Eurasia Center (Vakulenko); EIA; Goldman Sachs; Bloomberg; project knowledge verified revenue data

name: Sanctions Evasion Facilitation — SPFS and Shadow Fleet
description: Russia operates SPFS (System for Transfer of Financial Messages), its domestic alternative to SWIFT, which processes messaging for Russian financial institutions cut off from Western payment networks. SPFS has been integrated with China's CIPS (Cross-Border Interbank Payment System) to enable bilateral ruble-yuan settlements. Russia facilitates Iranian sanctions evasion through a shared shadow fleet of tankers that falsify GPS coordinates, change names, and use flags of convenience. This same fleet infrastructure that Russia built to evade its own oil sanctions since 2022 is directly transferable to supporting Iranian oil exports. As of January 2026, shadow tankers account for approximately 17% of global seaborne oil transport. Russia also provides Iran access to ruble-denominated trade channels and has explored BRICS Pay integration. These financial instruments allow Iran to continue exporting oil and importing goods outside the dollar-denominated system, though the scale remains limited compared to SWIFT volumes.
unit: SPFS messaging system; shadow fleet tanker network; BRICS Pay integration
political_cost: Sanctions evasion infrastructure is already built for Russia's own needs; extending it to Iran carries no additional political cost beyond what Russia already bears from its own sanctions status.
source: RUSI sanctions monitoring; Bloomberg; The Diplomat; Columbia CGEP; project knowledge BRICS/SPFS data

name: Ukraine War Commitment — Hard Constraint on Force Availability
description: As of January 2026, Russia has approximately 600,000–700,000 troops deployed in or supporting operations in Ukraine. Combined Russian and Ukrainian casualties may approach 2 million by spring 2026 per CSIS estimates, with Russian casualties assessed at 790,000–950,000 killed and wounded. Russia has been unable to form divisions to pre-2022 mechanized templates due to wartime equipment losses and insufficient AFV production, instead optimizing for massed infantry assaults in positional warfare. ISW assesses Russia is unlikely to generate a surplus of over 70,000 troops in 2026 for strategic reserves. Russia's GDP grew only 1% in 2025 (down from 4.3% in 2024), and federal budget spending is exceeding planned levels with a potential 2.2 trillion ruble shortfall. Russian forces advanced at an average of 15–70 meters per day in their most prominent offensives — slower than almost any major campaign in the last century. This means Russia has zero capacity for direct military intervention in an Iran conflict. It cannot spare troops, armor, air defense systems, or precision munitions from the Ukraine front.
unit: approximate deployed personnel in Ukraine theater; 70 meters/day average advance rate
political_cost: Direct Russian military involvement in an Iran conflict is not plausible under any scenario short of a US/NATO attack on Russian territory; Russia's entire conventional force is committed to Ukraine with no strategic reserve.
source: CSIS 'Russia's Grinding War in Ukraine' (Jan 2026); ISW daily assessments; Critical Threats; Russia Matters report card

name: Strategic Interest — US Distraction and NATO Attention Division
description: An Iran-US conflict represents Russia's optimal strategic scenario for the Ukraine war. It diverts US military assets (carrier strike groups, THAAD batteries, Patriot interceptors, precision munitions) from potential Ukraine support to the Middle East. The US had already supplied 10 Patriot systems to Ukraine before any Iran conflict, and produces only 60–65 Patriot missiles per month — any large-scale Middle East consumption directly reduces availability for Ukraine. A conflict also diverts Western political attention, making it harder for European allies to sustain Ukraine support packages. Oil price spikes fund Russia's war machine while weakening European economies dependent on energy imports. Russia's incentive structure is therefore strongly aligned toward prolonging any Iran-US conflict through intelligence support and diplomatic obstruction while avoiding direct involvement. This is the single most important motivation to model: Russia benefits maximally from a long, grinding Iran-US conflict that it supports indirectly.
unit: strategic alignment score: high
political_cost: Russia faces no political cost for passively benefiting; the calculation shifts only if the conflict ends so quickly that oil prices normalize before Russia captures meaningful revenue, or if the US pivots to pressuring Russia directly.
source: ISW; CSIS; RAND; project knowledge strategic assessment

name: Hard Ceiling — What Russia Will Not Do Even If Iran Is Losing
description: Russia will not commit conventional military forces to an Iran conflict under any plausible scenario. Russia has no expeditionary capability available — its entire ground force, most of its air force, and the bulk of its naval assets are committed to Ukraine or homeland defense. Russia will not transfer complete S-400 batteries, Su-57s, Iskander systems, or other top-tier systems that it needs for its own war. Russia will not provide nuclear guarantees or extended deterrence to Iran. Russia will not conduct strikes against US forces or assets. The maximum escalation Russia would consider is expanding intelligence sharing to include real-time tactical targeting data, increasing drone technology transfer, providing diplomatic cover at the UNSC, and potentially accelerating Su-35 deliveries from production lines. Even if Iran were losing badly, Russia's calculus would be that a weakened but surviving Iran still serves Russian interests better than risking a direct confrontation with the US while already fighting in Ukraine.
unit: constraint ceiling
political_cost: The threshold for exceeding this ceiling would require a direct US threat to Russian strategic assets or territory — a scenario that would trigger nuclear, not conventional, escalation logic and is outside the Iran conflict frame.
source: RAND; RUSI; ISW escalation analysis; project knowledge hard ceiling assessment

name: Cyber Collaboration with Iranian Groups
description: Pro-Russian hacktivist groups have established operational collaboration with Iranian cyber units, particularly against Israeli targets. This includes coordinated DDoS campaigns, shared malware toolkits, and joint targeting of critical infrastructure. Russian cyber actors (including GRU-affiliated groups) and Iranian actors (APT33, APT35, CyberAv3ngers) operate in overlapping target spaces and have been observed sharing techniques and infrastructure since at least 2023. In a conflict scenario, Russia could amplify Iranian cyber operations against US and allied networks without attribution, providing plausible deniability. The Symantec discovery of pre-installed Iranian backdoors on US company networks suggests a prepared cyber battlefield. Russia's contribution would be in sophistication escalation — Russian cyber capabilities are assessed as more advanced than Iranian ones, particularly in targeting industrial control systems and military communications.
unit: operational cyber collaboration; shared infrastructure and toolkits
political_cost: Cyber operations are inherently deniable; Russia would escalate cyber support only if it believed US attribution capabilities were insufficient to trigger proportional retaliation against Russian infrastructure.
source: Symantec; Mandiant; CISA advisories; project knowledge cyber assessment

name: Iranian Oil Imports — Economic Lifeline
description: China imported 1.38 million barrels per day of Iranian crude in 2025, absorbing 80–90% of Iran's total oil exports and accounting for approximately 12–14% of China's total seaborne crude imports. Iranian crude trades at an $8–10 per barrel discount to Brent, saving Chinese refiners billions annually. Purchases are concentrated among independent 'teapot' refineries in Shandong province, while state-owned companies avoid direct imports to limit sanctions exposure. Shipments are routinely relabeled as Malaysian or Indonesian origin — China imported more 'Malaysian' crude (1.3 million bpd) than Malaysia produces (535,000 bpd). Payment is made in yuan via China's CIPS system, bypassing SWIFT entirely. In January–February 2026, Iran exported 2.16 mbd (highest since July 2018), almost entirely to China, as pre-conflict stockpiling surged. China holds approximately 1.2–1.4 billion barrels in strategic reserves (roughly 120 days of net imports) plus 40+ million barrels in floating storage, providing substantial buffer capacity.
unit: million barrels per day (2025 average); 12–14% of China's seaborne imports
political_cost: China has already absorbed the political cost of sanctions evasion and views its Iranian oil purchases as a sovereign right; the threshold for reducing purchases would be credible US secondary sanctions enforcement against major Chinese financial institutions, which the US has historically been unwilling to impose.
source: Kpler analytics; Columbia CGEP (Erica Downs); Bruegel; The Diplomat; Reuters; project knowledge verified import data

name: Yuan Settlement Mechanics and CIPS Infrastructure
description: Nearly all Iranian oil exports to China are settled in yuan via CIPS (Cross-Border Interbank Payment System), China's alternative to SWIFT. Iran-China bilateral trade operates almost entirely outside the dollar system. The 2021 Iran-China 25-year cooperation agreement secures $400 billion in discounted oil for China in exchange for infrastructure investment and security cooperation. Russia-China oil trade has also shifted significantly to yuan — $19.14 billion in crude oil was settled in yuan in 2025, with over 20% of China's Russian oil imports yuan-denominated. The yuan's share of global oil payments remained below 5% as of mid-2025, confined mainly to bilateral deals with sanctioned states (Russia, Iran, Venezuela). CIPS processed transactions linked to the mBridge digital currency platform, which handled over $55.5 billion by November 2025 with digital yuan comprising 95%. While operationally significant for Iran, this infrastructure does not yet threaten dollar dominance — the USD still facilitates 80–90% of global oil transactions.
unit: CIPS system; $400B 25-year cooperation agreement; <5% global oil payment share
political_cost: Yuan settlement for Iranian oil is already priced into China-US relations; China would consider expanding yuan oil settlement to Gulf states only if a Hormuz crisis created sufficient pressure, and even then faces resistance from dollar-pegged economies like Saudi Arabia.
source: Columbia CGEP; Bruegel; SCMP; project knowledge petrodollar/yuan assessment

name: Belt and Road Investment and Iran Destabilization Risk
description: Iran is a key node in China's Belt and Road Initiative, sitting at the crossroads of the China-Central Asia-West Asia corridor. The 2021 25-year cooperation agreement encompasses Chinese investment in Iranian ports (Chabahar competitor strategies), rail links, telecommunications, and energy infrastructure. Iran's geographic position is critical for BRI land routes bypassing maritime chokepoints — precisely the vulnerability that a Hormuz closure would highlight. If Iran destabilizes or experiences regime collapse, China risks losing: (a) $400 billion in committed energy deals, (b) BRI infrastructure investments, (c) a key land-route transit state, and (d) a strategic partner in balancing US influence in the Middle East. However, China has hedged by deepening ties with Gulf states, Pakistan, and Central Asian republics. China's calculation is that a surviving, weakened Iran that remains dependent on Chinese economic support is preferable to either regime change (unpredictable successor) or a pro-Western Iran (lost leverage).
unit: billion USD (25-year cooperation agreement value)
political_cost: BRI investments create sunk-cost pressure to support Iran's survival but not at the cost of China's far more valuable trade relationships with the US and EU; China would accept Iranian territorial losses or diminished sovereignty before risking its own economic position.
source: CSIS BRI database; Bruegel; IISS Strategic Survey; project knowledge BRI/economic data

name: SCO Membership and UNSC Coordination
description: China coordinates with Russia at the UNSC to block Western-sponsored resolutions on Iran, providing a reliable second veto. China also leverages Iran's SCO membership (full member since 2023) to frame any US military action as aggression against a member of a major multilateral organization. China's diplomatic strategy combines UNSC vetoes, SCO solidarity statements, and Global South framing — positioning US-Iran conflict as a continuation of Western imperialism and violations of sovereignty. Foreign Minister Wang Yi's condemnation of the June 2025 strikes as lacking UN authorization established the template. China also deploys special envoys for mediation (Zhai Jun conducted a regional tour in early 2026), positioning itself as a responsible peacemaker while Russia plays the spoiler role. This diplomatic division of labor is deliberate: China maintains credibility with Gulf states and the EU as a potential mediator while Russia provides harder-edged support.
unit: permanent UNSC vetoes (Russia + China); SCO full membership
political_cost: UNSC vetoes coordinated with Russia are low-cost and align with China's sovereignty narrative; the threshold for breaking from Russia on Iran would be if UNSC cooperation began to damage China's relationships with Gulf oil suppliers.
source: UN records; SCO communiqués; IISS; project knowledge diplomatic assessment

name: Constraints on Direct Military Intervention
description: Direct Chinese military intervention in an Iran-US conflict is implausible for four reinforcing reasons. First, China's economic interdependence with the US ($700+ billion in bilateral trade) means any military confrontation would trigger catastrophic economic consequences far exceeding any benefit from supporting Iran. Second, Taiwan remains China's primary military planning priority — PLA force structure, naval construction, and amphibious capabilities are oriented toward a potential Taiwan contingency, not Middle East expeditionary operations. Third, China has no meaningful blue-water navy presence in the Persian Gulf; its naval base in Djibouti supports anti-piracy operations, not combat power projection into contested waters defended by multiple US carrier strike groups. Fourth, China's 2026 defense budget increased 7% to approximately $278 billion, but this is allocated to Taiwan-oriented capabilities (anti-access/area denial, amphibious forces, naval expansion), not Middle East expeditionary capacity. China's military strategy is fundamentally defensive and regional, not expeditionary.
unit: billion USD (2026 defense budget); zero combat vessels in Persian Gulf
political_cost: Direct military involvement would end China's economic relationship with the US and EU, cost trillions in trade disruption, and jeopardize Taiwan planning; no Iran scenario reaches this threshold.
source: IISS Military Balance 2026; PLA budget announcements; CSIS China Power Project; project knowledge military constraint data

name: Technology Transfers — Dual-Use and Military Systems
description: China has provided Iran with several militarily significant technology transfers, though the exact scope is contested. Confirmed transfers include: full military access to the BeiDou-3 encrypted satellite navigation system (granted 2021), which Iran uses for precision guidance on ballistic missiles and drones; radar systems and navigation technology; electronic warfare tools; and approximately 3,000 tons of sodium perchlorate (solid rocket fuel precursor), enough to produce hundreds of ballistic missiles. Pre-war reports (Middle East Eye, February 2026) cited three officials claiming China delivered loitering munitions and air defense systems before the conflict. Reuters reported Iran was 'nearing completion' of a deal for CM-302 supersonic anti-ship missiles, though China's MFA denied this. The most credible assessment (ISW/AEI) is that China provided limited pre-war transfers of dual-use and some military systems, with negotiations ongoing for larger deals, but has not conducted confirmed large-scale military resupply.
unit: tons sodium perchlorate (confirmed); BeiDou-3 access (confirmed); CM-302 missiles (unconfirmed/denied)
political_cost: Confirmed military transfers to Iran would trigger US secondary sanctions on Chinese defense firms and potentially broader economic retaliation; China maintains deniability through dual-use framing and MFA denials, and the threshold for overt military supply would require a complete breakdown in China-US relations.
source: ISW/AEI; Reuters; Middle East Eye; Columbia CGEP; project knowledge verified transfer data

name: Taiwan Priority — Why China De-escalates During Iran Crises
description: China's behavior during Iran crises reveals its strategic priorities. In late February 2026, Taiwan recorded 13 consecutive days without Chinese warplanes flying near the island — described as unprecedented. PLA ADIZ incursions dropped below 200/month in January–February 2026, down from 300+/month average. ISW/AEI assessed China is 'not looking to ramp up tensions with Taiwan' during Middle East crises, prioritizing the planned Trump-Xi summit and avoiding giving the US a pretext for redirecting attention. However, China continues long-term preparations: December 2025 'Justice Mission' drills rehearsed a full maritime blockade of Taiwan; the 2026 government work report strengthened anti-independence language; and coast guard incursions near Kinmen continued. The key insight for the simulation: China will exploit the intelligence windfall from US THAAD/Patriot redeployments (gaining data on US systems removed from South Korea) but will not militarily exploit the Taiwan window, preferring to accumulate advantages for a future contingency on its own timeline.
unit: strategic patience posture; 13-day warplane pause near Taiwan
political_cost: Exploiting the Taiwan window during an Iran crisis would sacrifice China's most valuable strategic asset — time — and trigger the exact US/allied military response that China's entire defense strategy is designed to avoid; only a Taiwanese declaration of independence would override this calculus.
source: ISW/AEI; PLATracker (Ben Lewis); CSIS; project knowledge Taiwan assessment

name: Strategic Oil Reserves and Crisis Buffer Capacity
description: China has invested heavily in strategic resilience against precisely the kind of energy shock an Iran-US conflict would produce. As of early January 2026, China held approximately 1.206 billion barrels of oil in onshore storage, covering 104 days of net crude imports at 2025 levels. By March 2, this grew to 1.39 billion barrels (120 days coverage). Additionally, over 46 million barrels of Iranian oil sit in floating storage in Asia and in bonded storage at Chinese ports (Dalian and Zhoushan, where NIOC leases tanks). China's total crude imports hit a record 11.6 million bpd in 2025, with 430,000 bpd going to stockpiling (83% of the import increase). China also diversified sources aggressively — increasing Brazilian imports 28% and securing additional overland Russian pipeline flows. This reserve capacity means China can weather a multi-month disruption of its Middle Eastern imports, fundamentally reducing its vulnerability to Strait of Hormuz closure and diminishing US leverage.
unit: million barrels onshore storage (Jan 2026); 104 days import coverage; 46M barrels floating/bonded
political_cost: Stockpiling is a purely defensive measure with no political cost; it signals China's expectation of conflict but does not constitute support for either side.
source: Columbia CGEP (Erica Downs); Kayrros; Rystad Energy; Bruegel; project knowledge reserve data

name: Dual Supply Chain — Drone Components and Industrial Inputs
description: Iran's drone and missile production relies heavily on Chinese-origin commercial components, creating a dual-use supply chain that is difficult to sanction without disrupting legitimate trade. Iran's Shahed-136 drones use commercially available Chinese components including GPS modules, microprocessors, and engine parts. Iran's ballistic missile program depends on Chinese-origin sodium perchlorate for solid rocket fuel — approximately 3,000 tons were shipped in 2025, enough for hundreds of missiles. Two Iranian ships departed China carrying sodium perchlorate in early March 2026. The drone component supply chain operates through commercial trading companies and transshipment networks that are structurally difficult to interdict. China maintains plausible deniability by classifying these as commercial sales to end-users that may divert them. This supply chain is arguably more strategically significant than any direct military transfer because it sustains Iran's asymmetric production capacity at scale.
unit: tons sodium perchlorate (2025); commercial drone components (ongoing)
political_cost: Component supply chains are commercially deniable and operate below the threshold of formal military transfers; China would reduce flows only under direct, credible US secondary sanctions targeting specific Chinese firms, and even then enforcement is extremely difficult.
source: Bloomberg; CSIS; Reuters; ISW/AEI; project knowledge supply chain data

name: Escalation Threshold — When China Would Increase Support vs. Accept Iranian Defeat
description: China's threshold calculus for escalating support to Iran versus quietly accepting an Iranian defeat is the most important variable to model. China would escalate support (financial assistance, accelerated component shipments, expanded BeiDou access, possible CM-302 delivery) under the following conditions: (a) Strait of Hormuz remains closed for 3+ months, threatening China's 45–50% oil import dependency via the Strait; (b) US imposes a naval blockade that explicitly targets Chinese-flagged vessels; (c) the conflict visibly weakens US military posture in the Indo-Pacific, creating opportunities for long-term advantage. China would accept Iranian defeat and prioritize preserving the China-US relationship under: (a) a quick US military victory that restores Hormuz transit within weeks; (b) US offers a credible summit or trade deal that makes accommodation more valuable than Iranian partnership; (c) a successor Iranian regime that maintains commercial oil relationships with China. The critical dynamic is that China's support is conditional and transactional — Iran is a useful partner, not an ally worth sacrificing core economic interests for.
unit: conditional escalation framework
political_cost: China's maximum escalation — overt military supply — would only occur if the US had already fundamentally disrupted China-US economic relations through actions like a naval blockade of Chinese shipping, at which point the relationship would already be broken; short of that, China calibrates support to stay below the threshold of US retaliation.
source: Bruegel; ISW/AEI; CSIS; RAND; Columbia CGEP; project knowledge threshold analysis
